question: Explain the difference between entity set and entity instance with an example.
expectedPoints: Entity set is a collection of entities of the same type (the schema/blueprint)
Entity instance is a single, specific occurrence of an entity (the actual data)
Example: Student is entity set, while 'John Smith, Roll No. 101' is an entity instance

question: What is a composite attribute? Give an example and show how it is represented in ER diagram.
expectedPoints: Composite attribute can be divided into smaller sub-attributes
Example: Address can be split into Street, City, State, Pincode
Represented as ovals connected to the entity with lines to sub-attribute ovals

question: Differentiate between total participation and partial participation with examples.
expectedPoints: Total participation (mandatory): Every entity must participate in relationship
Partial participation (optional): Entity may or may not participate
Example: In Library system, every Book must be borrowed by some Member (total), but a Member may not borrow any book (partial)

question: What is a weak entity? How is it different from a strong entity? Explain with an example.
expectedPoints: Weak entity does not have primary key of its own, depends on strong entity
Strong entity has its own primary key, exists independently
Example: Dependent is weak entity (depends on Employee), Employee is strong entity
Weak entity represented by double rectangle, identified by double diamond relationship

question: Explain the three types of cardinality in ER diagrams with one example each.
expectedPoints: One-to-One (1:1): One entity relates to exactly one other entity
One-to-Many (1:M): One entity relates to many other entities
Many-to-Many (M:N): Many entities relate to many other entities
Examples: Person-Aadhaar (1:1), Department-Employees (1:M), Student-Courses (M:N)

question: What is an associative entity? When is it needed in ER modeling?
expectedPoints: Associative entity converts M:N relationship into two 1:M relationships
It is needed when we have a Many-to-Many relationship between entities
It inherits primary keys from related entities to form its own composite key
Example: In Student-Course enrollment, Enrollment is associative entity

question: Define the following terms: (i) Key Attribute (ii) Multi-valued Attribute (iii) Derived Attribute
expectedPoints: Key Attribute: Uniquely identifies each entity instance, underlined in ER diagram
Multi-valued Attribute: Can have multiple values for one entity, shown with double oval
Derived Attribute: Value can be computed from other attributes, shown with dashed oval

question: Draw an ER diagram for a Hospital Management System. The system has Patients, Doctors, and Wards. A patient is admitted to one ward and is treated by one doctor. A doctor can treat multiple patients. A ward has multiple patients. Identify all entities, attributes, relationships, and specify cardinality and participation constraints.
expectedPoints: Entities: Patient (Patient_ID, Name, Age, Gender, Address, Phone), Doctor (Doctor_ID, Name, Specialization, Experience), Ward (Ward_No, Ward_Name, Capacity, Type)
Relationships: Admitted (Patient-Ward), Treats (Doctor-Patient)
Cardinality: Patient-Ward is 1:1, Doctor-Patient is 1:M, Ward-Patient is 1:M
Participation: Patient has total participation in Admitted, partial in Treats; Doctor partial in Treats; Ward total in Admitted
Key attributes: Patient_ID, Doctor_ID, Ward_No respectively

question: Explain the Entity-Relationship (ER) Model developed by Peter Chen. Discuss its components and their symbols in ER diagrams. Also explain why ER modeling is important in database design.
expectedPoints: Peter Chen developed ER model in 1976 for conceptual database design
Components: Entities (objects), Attributes (properties), Relationships (associations)
Symbols: Rectangle for entities, Oval for attributes, Diamond for relationships
Importance: Provides graphical representation, helps in communication, bridges gap between user view and database implementation, forms foundation for relational schema

question: Design an ER diagram for an Online Shopping System. The system has Customers, Products, Orders, and Categories. Customers can place multiple orders. Each order contains multiple products. Products belong to one category. Draw the ER diagram with all attributes, relationships, cardinality, and participation constraints.
expectedPoints: Entities: Customer (Cust_ID, Name, Email, Phone, Address), Product (Prod_ID, Name, Price, Description, Stock), Order (Order_ID, Date, Total_Amount, Status), Category (Cat_ID, Name, Description)
Relationships: Places (Customer-Order, 1:M), Contains (Order-Product, M:N - needs associative entity), Belongs (Product-Category, M:1)
Key attributes identified for each entity
Participation constraints: Customer partial in Places, Order total in Contains, Product partial in Belongs

question: What is the difference between binary, ternary, and quaternary relationships? Explain with examples. Also discuss when to use each type.
expectedPoints: Binary relationship: Involves 2 entities (Student-Course)
Ternary relationship: Involves 3 entities (Student-Course-Instructor)
Quaternary relationship: Involves 4 entities
Choice depends on business rules: Binary for simple associations, ternary when three entities must be related simultaneously
Example of ternary: A project involves Employee working on Project using Skill - all three must be considered together
